did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and ensemble learning methods to classify skin cancer. Specifically, it employed transfer learning with pretrained models (MobileNetv3small, Nasnet-Mobile, and EfficientNetv2-B0) and combined them using a stacking ensemble strategy with a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) meta-learner to classify basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma from ISIC dataset images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a stacking ensemble deep learning model using MobileNetv3small, Nasnet-Mobile, and EfficientNetv2-B0 to classify skin cancer from the ISIC dataset. It includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Results (with accuracy metrics like 99.90%), and a Conclusion, and is published in the proceedings of the 14th International Conference on Computing Communication and Networking Technologies (ICCCNT).

title: An Ensemble Learning Approach For Improved Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2023 14th International Conference on Computing Communication and Networking Technologies (ICCCNT)
brief_summary: This study developed a stacking ensemble deep learning model to classify three primary skin cancer types: basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma. The researchers utilized a balanced dataset of 6,385 images per class from the ISIC archive, which was augmented and resized to 224x224 pixels. The ensemble combined three transfer learning models—MobileNetv3small, Nasnet-Mobile, and EfficientNetv2-B0—using a Multi-layer Perceptron as a meta-learner. Individual models achieved test accuracies of 98.48%, 99.32%, and 98.59%, respectively. The proposed stacking ensemble model achieved a superior test accuracy of 99.90%, outperforming the individual architectures and existing studies. The results demonstrate that the ensemble approach significantly improves classification performance for early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three malignant subtypes studied. Filtered from the ISIC Archive dataset (432 original images, augmented to 6,385 images). Classified using the proposed ensemble model with 100% recall and 0.996875 precision.

One of the three malignant subtypes studied. Filtered from the ISIC Archive dataset (212 original images, augmented to 6,385 images). Classified using the proposed ensemble model with 0.998433 recall and 100% precision.
One of the three malignant subtypes studied. Filtered from the ISIC Archive dataset (6,385 original images). Classified using the proposed ensemble model with 0.998433 recall and 100% precision.

all_task_types.target: Three primary skin cancer types: basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and melanoma.
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using a stacking ensemble deep learning model. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC, SCC, Melanoma) from dermoscopic images. The final layer uses softmax activation to generate class probabilities for these three classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The study filtered this dataset to extract images of three malignant skin cancer subtypes. The original dataset contained 75,010 images, from which 7,029 images were selected for this specific study.

use.training: Used to train the individual transfer learning models (MobileNetv3small, Nasnet-Mobile, EfficientNetv2-B0) and the stacking ensemble model. The dataset was split into an 80:10:10 ratio for train, validation, and test sets, resulting in 15,324 training, 1,917 validation, and 1,914 testing images.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping (validation loss not reducing after 5 epochs).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the individual models and the ensemble model (test accuracies of 98.48%, 99.32%, 98.59%, and 99.90% respectively).
use.other: The dataset was augmented (rotation, shifting, shearing, zooming, flipping, reflection fill mode) and resized to 224x224 pixels to create a balanced dataset of 6,385 images per class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, with comparisons made to existing studies based on these metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive.description: Dataset filtered to 7,029 images of three malignant subtypes (BCC, SCC, Melanoma), balanced to 6,385 images per class via augmentation. Split into 80:10:10 ratio for train (15,324), validation (1,917), and test (1,914) sets.
ISIC Archive.notes: The stacking ensemble model outperformed individual models and existing studies. Nasnet-Mobile achieved the highest metrics among individual models. The ensemble model used a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) meta-learner with three dense layers (256 units each) and Adam optimizer.

architecture_used.main_model: Stacking Ensemble of MobileNetv3small, Nasnet-Mobile, and EfficientNetv2-B0 with a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP) meta-learner

MobileNetv3small.features: Depthwise separable convolutions, linear bottleneck blocks, Hard Swish activation

Nasnet-Mobile.features: Normal and reduction cells, feature reuse mechanism, progressive learning

EfficientNetv2-B0.features: Not specified in detail beyond name

training_details.early_stopping: Stopped if validation loss did not reduce after 5 epochs

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation techniques including rotation, shifting, shearing, zooming, flipping, and reflection fill mode
Cropping augmented images to remove borders, resulting in a final size of 224x224 pixels
Application of Gaussian blur with a kernel size of 3x3
Image normalization to scale pixel values between 0 and 1

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, shifting, shearing, zooming, and flipping were applied to the images.
Reflection fill mode was used during augmentation.
Gaussian blur with a kernel size of 3x3 was applied to the images.
Images were augmented to create a balanced dataset of 6,385 images per class (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, and Melanoma) from original counts of 432, 212, and 6,385 respectively.
Augmented images were cropped to remove borders, resulting in a final size of 224x224 pixels.

conclusions: The proposed stacking ensemble deep learning model, combining MobileNetv3small, Nasnet-Mobile, and EfficientNetv2-B0 with a Multi-layer Perceptron meta-learner, achieved a test accuracy of 99.90% in classifying basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma.
The ensemble model outperformed the individual transfer learning models, which achieved test accuracies of 98.48% (MobileNetv3small), 99.32% (Nasnet-Mobile), and 98.59% (EfficientNetv2-B0).

The proposed method demonstrated superior performance compared to existing studies, including those using InceptionV3 (86.90%), weighted average ensemble (88.00%), and noise removal with deep CNN (91.93%).
Nasnet-Mobile was identified as the most effective individual model among the three tested architectures.
The study confirms that ensemble learning strategies can significantly improve classification accuracy for skin cancer detection compared to individual models.
The authors acknowledge that future work may extend this approach to classify a larger number of skin lesion classes encountered in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study relies on a highly artificial dataset balance achieved by augmenting Basal Cell Carcinoma (432 original images) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (212 original images) to match Melanoma (6,385 original images), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the performance improvements over individual models or existing works.

External validation is limited; comparisons with existing studies are made against heterogeneous datasets with different class distributions (e.g., 2-class vs. 7-class problems) and image counts, making direct performance comparison difficult.
The model was trained and tested on a single source (ISIC Archive), raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices without external validation.

The reported test accuracy of 99.90% is exceptionally high for skin cancer classification, which may indicate potential data leakage or overfitting given the synthetic class balancing and limited external benchmarking.

The study focuses only on three malignant subtypes (BCC, SCC, Melanoma) and excludes benign lesions, limiting its immediate clinical utility for differential diagnosis in a general dermatology setting.
